indicators.name: Cumulative-Effects Infringement Standard — Post-Yahey
indicators.summary: Yahey (2021 BCSC 1287) lowered the bar to 'significant or meaningful diminishment' and made the regulatory regime itself reviewable.
indicators.value: Lowered threshold (BC); being tested in AB/SK/ON
indicators.note: Previously Mikisew (2005 SCC 69) required 'no meaningful exercise'. Yahey lowers that to 'significant or meaningful diminishment' and aggregates cumulative effects of many prior approvals.

indicators.name: Blueberry River Claim Area — Industrial Disturbance Footprint
indicators.summary: By September 2018, 85% of the ~38,000 km² Blueberry Claim Area was within 250 m of an industrial disturbance; 91% within 500 m.
indicators.note: Factual basis on which Justice Burke found unjustified Treaty 8 infringement in Yahey.

indicators.name: Blueberry River Implementation Agreement — Restoration Fund Target
indicators.summary: CAD $200 million target by 2025, funded by $60,000/ha disturbance fees plus $87.5M direct over 3 years and royalty share.
indicators.note: Plus ~350,000 m³/yr Fort St. John TSA forestry reduction; PNG no-go and 50% reduction zones; Site C transmission carveout from 'New Disturbance' definition.

indicators.name: Five-Nations Treaty 8 Settlement Quantum
indicators.summary: Federal + BC governments paid $800M in April 2023 to settle land claims across Blueberry River, Doig River, Halfway River, Saulteau, West Moberly.
indicators.note: Signals that the Yahey precedent reaches well beyond Blueberry River alone.

indicators.name: Alberta Priority-Project Regulatory Approval Target
indicators.summary: Alberta Indigenous Relations Business Plan 2026-29 commits the Ministry to support a 120-day approval timeline for priority projects.
indicators.note: Risk indicator: fast-track timelines compress the space for adequate consultation and FPIC. Compare against AB consultation guide Level 3 maximum of 60 working days for 'exploring concerns'.

sources.citing: Ministry will support 120-day priority project approval timeline

indicators.name: Alberta Energy Regulator 'Directly and Adversely Affected' Standard
indicators.summary: AER held a First Nation with reserve within 15 km of a proposed fracking development was not 'directly and adversely affected'; Samson Cree denied appeal for lack of 'hard evidence of presence in some ascertainable proximity'.
indicators.note: REDA s.34 leaves the decision to hold a hearing to AER discretion; ABCA review only on questions of law or jurisdiction.

indicators.name: Alberta Water Act — Silence on Section 35 Rights
indicators.summary: The AB Water Act (RSA 2000, c W-3) contains zero occurrences of 'Aboriginal', 'Indigenous', 'First Nation', or 'Treaty' — the entire Indigenous-rights overlay sits outside the Act.
indicators.note: Water Act last substantively amended 1999. Treaty rights to water (where established) flow through Section 35 and common law, not statute.

sources.citing: Water Act, RSA 2000, c W-3 (full-text grep)
Tsuu T'ina Nation v. Alberta, 2008 ABQB 547

indicators.name: Federal Major-Project Consultation — Heightened Scrutiny
indicators.summary: After the 2018 FCA decision in Tsleil-Waututh quashed the original TMX approval, second-round consultation processes became the working norm for federally-led major projects.

indicators.name: South Saskatchewan River Basin — Closure Status
indicators.summary: Closed to most new allocations since 2007 (AR 171/2007); reserved water allocatable only for First Nation use on Reserve, WCOs, aquatic-environment storage, or pre-closure applications.
indicators.note: First Nation on-Reserve use is one of only four explicit exceptions — the most-protected modern pathway for new Indigenous-led water development in southern Alberta.

indicators.name: St. Mary River — Allocation vs. Median Natural Flow
indicators.summary: Existing allocations stand at ≈ 118% of median natural flow on the St. Mary (Belly 80%; Waterton 75%).
indicators.note: Structural over-allocation pre-dating the 2007 SSRB closure; persists as a baseline scarcity factor.

indicators.name: Alberta Drought Stage — Current Reading
indicators.summary: As of Dec 31 2025: range S0 (Adequate) to S3 (Severe) across basins; most basins improved after December precipitation.
indicators.note: Five-stage system (S0 Adequate → S5 Catastrophic); S5 corresponds to 1-in-50-year event.

indicators.name: 2026 Mountain Runoff Forecast (% of Median)
indicators.summary: Mar-Sep 2026 forecasts: NSRB 108–119%; Red Deer 118–128%; Bow 97–116%; Oldman 99–117%; Milk 82–113% — all above 2025 observed.

indicators.name: Wapiti / Smoky Sub-basin — Oil & Gas Water Allocation Utilization
indicators.summary: Oil & gas water-allocation utilization ≈ 99.64% of allocated volume; projected +62.79% by 2050 under reference scenarios.
indicators.note: Pressure point in northern Alberta for upstream Indigenous communities. Lower Peace livestock currently overuses allocation by 468%.

indicators.name: Aboriginal Extreme Flow (AXF) — Lower Athabasca
indicators.summary: AXF threshold = 482 m³/s. Number of days below AXF in fall has increased since 1990 — directly impacts Indigenous navigation rights along the Lower Athabasca.
indicators.value: 482 m³/s threshold; breach days rising in fall

sources.citing: OSM 2025 §7 Water Levels and Indigenous Travel, Figure 14

indicators.name: Alberta Wetland Loss — Settled Area (historical)
indicators.summary: ~Two-thirds of wetlands lost in Alberta's settled area since the 1800s; in one 4,405 km² central-AB study area, > 80% of 1999–2009 loss occurred without permit.

indicators.name: Alberta Mine Watersheds — Climate Stress (2050s, RCP8.5 p50)
indicators.summary: Snow depth declines 36–42%; current 100-yr 24-h IDF storm becomes a ~37–47-yr event; temperature +2.4–3.2°C.
indicators.note: Double-bind: heavier short-duration storms (more overflow/tailings risk) plus much less snowpack (less summer baseflow).

indicators.name: AESO Large-Load (Data Centre) Interconnection Queue
indicators.summary: Year-end 2025: > 20,000 MW of potential load in the connection process — nearly double Alberta's average demand of ~10,500 MW.
indicators.note: Applications, not approvals. June 2025 Phase I snapshot: 29 data-centre applications totalling 16,229 MW.

sources.citing: AESO 2025 Annual Report — Data Centres and Reliability Standards

indicators.name: AESO Phase I Interim Connection Limit (2027/2028)
indicators.summary: 1,200 MW reliability-based interim cap. Only two projects elected to proceed under the cap — Phase 1 formally concluded early November 2025.
indicators.note: AESO frames 1,200 MW as 'the equivalent of 10 per cent to the province's current peak electricity use'.

indicators.name: Alberta Winter Peak Demand (2025, record)
indicators.summary: New all-time winter peak in 2025: 12,785 MW (vs. Jan 2024 peak of 12,384 MW).
indicators.note: 2025 internal-load growth 2.0% vs five-year average 1.5% — pre-data-centre baseline.

sources.citing: AESO 2025 Annual Report — By the Numbers

indicators.name: AESO Transmission Must-Run (TMR) Costs — 2025 Spike
indicators.summary: TMR costs $21.3M in 2025 (+301.9% YoY). TCR (Transmission Constraint Rebalancing) costs $14.4M (+278.9% YoY).
indicators.note: Increase reflects more events requiring TMR in the northwest area (Treaty 8 corridor) and renewables-curtailment congestion.

sources.citing: AESO 2025 Annual Report — Other Ancillary Services

indicators.name: NERC — Observed Voltage-Sensitive Large-Load Reduction Events
indicators.summary: Eastern and Texas Interconnections each experienced roughly 1,500 MW of voltage-sensitive load reduction events tied to emerging large loads.
indicators.note: Empirical basis behind AESO's Phase I reliability caution. AESO committed to adopt forthcoming NERC large-load standards in Phase II.

indicators.name: Capital Power Genesee Data Centre — Proposed
indicators.summary: Up to 1,500 MW proposed (SASR filed June 2024) on Capital Power land within Genesee Mine Permit Area (Treaty 6 territory). Did not receive sufficient Phase 1 allocation; deferred to Phase 2 BYOG framework.
indicators.note: Capital Power newsletter does not mention Treaty 6 status, First Nation consultation, or impact-benefit framework.

indicators.name: Alberta AI Data Centre Strategy — Disclosed Targets
indicators.summary: The strategy itself discloses NO dollar target, NO MW target, NO timeline, NO Treaty-territory siting analysis.
indicators.note: Important corrective: widely-circulated 'CAD $100B by year X' framing is not present in the strategy document itself. Treaty-Lab will only report what the source actually documents.

sources.citing: GoA AI DC Strategy, full document

indicators.name: CIB Indigenous Equity Initiative — Per-Deal Terms
indicators.summary: Loans CAD $5M–$100M, up to 90% of Indigenous equity interest. Pricing matched to Government of Canada bond yield + 75% cash sweep on equity distributions; tenor target 15 years.

indicators.name: Federal CILGC Indigenous Loan Guarantee Program — Per-Deal Range
indicators.summary: Per-deal $20M minimum, $1B maximum. Sector-agnostic except gaming. Federal stacking cap: combined federal support (incl. CIB + CILGC + other federal Crowns) cannot exceed 75% of project costs.
indicators.note: Eligibility limited to Indigenous groups with credibly asserted or affirmed Section 35 rights.

indicators.name: FNFA Loan Portfolio Size and Credit Rating
indicators.summary: Loan portfolio ≈ CAD $3.9 billion (Aug 2025), up from $2.5B prior year. Credit ratings: S&P AA- (upgraded from A+); Moody's Aa3; Morningstar DBRS AA (low) — all Stable.
indicators.note: Largest single-borrower concentration ≈ 10.3% of portfolio. Untested DRF replenishment and intervention mechanisms remain the key structural risk.

indicators.name: Projected First Nation Equity Need 2023–2033
indicators.summary: FNMPC projects ~CAD $51 billion in Indigenous equity will require financing support across natural-resource major projects 2023–2033.

indicators.name: First Nations Infrastructure Gap — FNFA-cited estimate
indicators.summary: FNFA cites an infrastructure gap First Nations face of ≈ CAD $349.2 billion — over and above what current FNFA financing opportunities can address.

sources.citing: FNFA Annual Report — Message from the President and CEO

indicators.name: Coastal GasLink — Documented First Nation Equity Forfeiture
indicators.summary: FNMPC documents that 'some First Nations attempted to acquire an additional 10% in a now-completed financing round and had to forfeit their opportunity to their private-sector partners as they were unable to successfully raise capital at rates that made the equity investment financially viable.'
indicators.note: Forfeiture occurred before federal ILGP/CILGC existed (announced Fall 2023). Establishes baseline cost of inaction as a documented opportunity loss.

indicators.name: Hydro One Indigenous Equity Threshold
indicators.summary: Every new transmission line project over $100 million in Ontario offers applicable First Nations 50% equity. Equity earns the OEB-regulated rate of return (historically 8–10%).
indicators.note: Active comparator precedent for regulated-utility-anchored Indigenous equity (vs. commodity-exposure equity). Codified in Hydro One's signed Indigenous Relations Policy.

indicators.name: Westcoast NG Pipeline — Stonlasec8 Alliance Equity / CILGC First Major Guarantee
indicators.summary: Stonlasec8 Alliance Limited Partnership (38 Indigenous communities) acquired 12.5% in Westcoast Natural Gas Pipeline (July 2025) with a CAD $400M CILGC federal loan guarantee — the first major use of the federal ILGP.

indicators.name: Indigenous Pipeline Ownership Acquired Since 2021
indicators.summary: Indigenous Communities have acquired interests in > 5,000 km of operating pipelines in Canada since 2021. Indigenous Communities are the third-largest collective owners of clean energy assets nationally after government.

indicators.name: UNDRIP Act Implementation — Action Plan 2023-2028 Status
indicators.summary: Annual public reporting to Parliament (Measure 20); 5-year Action Plan review cycle (Measure 21); commitment to withdraw the Comprehensive Land Claims and Inherent Right Policies (Measure 23); FPIC-aligned guidance for natural-resource projects (Measure 32).

indicators.name: Federal Holdings of First Nations Data — Data-Bank Count
indicators.summary: AANDC (now Indigenous Services Canada / Crown-Indigenous Relations) alone holds 210+ data banks of First Nations data, including the Indian Registry System, NIHB, FN Child and Family Services, education nominal roll, and income assistance.
indicators.note: Access to Information Act allows de-identified First Nations community-aggregate data to be released to any requester over the objections of the First Nation.

indicators.name: Federal ATIP — First Nation 'Government' Recognition Gap
indicators.summary: Only self-governing or modern-treaty First Nations and 'Participating First Nations' under the BC Education Jurisdiction Act are recognized as 'governments' for confidential information sharing under federal ATIP.
indicators.value: Recognized only for self-government / modern treaty / BC Education FNs
indicators.note: Provincial parallel: only Nova Scotia and Alberta recognize FN governments as 'governments' for confidential info sharing. UNDRIP Act Measure 30 commits to addressing this through legislative, regulatory, and policy options.

indicators.name: Mohawk Council of Akwesasne — First-Nation Data Sovereignty Precedent
indicators.summary: Mohawk Council of Akwesasne is the only First Nation that holds its own NIHB and First Nation Health Information System data — enabled by enacting its own First Nation privacy law.
indicators.value: Only FN holding own NIHB + FNHIS data
indicators.note: Direct, replicable template for Treaty-Lab-style data governance: First Nation enacts its own privacy law, then federal-data-holders can transfer custody outside ATIP scope.

indicators.name: Alberta — First Nation Protocol / Relationship Agreement Coverage
indicators.summary: Active or in-development agreements with only 4 First Nations bodies: Blackfoot Confederacy (protocol); Stoney Nakoda–Tsuut'ina Tribal Council (protocol); Confederacy of Treaty Six (relationship); Treaty 8 MOU under development.
indicators.note: Most Alberta First Nations sit outside a formal protocol or relationship agreement. These are not equity agreements.

sources.citing: AB IR Business Plan Performance Measure 4.a
